title: Apple cider doughnuts
subtitle: Baked cake doughnuts with reduced apple juice and warm spices
excerpt: Apple cider doughnuts are an American autumn staple: baked cake doughnuts made with reduced apple juice for a concentrated apple flavour and warm spices throughout. They are faster to make than yeasted doughnuts and baked rather than fried. The reduction is the step that makes the difference.
sourceNotes: Apple cider doughnuts are a tradition at apple orchards and farm shops across New England and the mid-Atlantic states of America, typically available during the autumn apple harvest from September to November. The use of reduced apple cider (in the American sense, unfermented fresh apple juice) to concentrate the apple flavour is documented across New England baking texts. This recipe adapts the American tradition using readily available apple juice.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before coating in cinnamon sugar. Thaw at room temperature and coat just before serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day. The apple reduction can be made a day ahead.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan or conventional; a high temperature and a hot, greased tin give the doughnuts their crust

glossaryTerms.definition: Simmering apple juice until it reduces by half concentrates the natural sugars and apple flavour to give a far more pronounced apple taste than using juice at full strength. The reduction takes about 15 minutes. It must be completely cooled before going into the batter; warm reduction will start to activate the leavening early. The reduced juice will be thick, syrupy and very sweet.

content.text: is what makes these taste of apple rather than just of spiced cake. Do not skip it. The batter is a standard muffin-method cake batter; the doughnuts are baked in a muffin tin and then brushed with melted butter and rolled in cinnamon sugar while warm.

Pour the 300 ml apple juice into a small saucepan. Bring to the boil, then reduce the heat to a brisk simmer. Cook for 15 to 18 minutes until the juice has reduced by half to around 150 ml and is slightly syrupy. Pour into a bowl and cool completely, at least 30 minutes in the fridge.

Heat the oven to 200 degrees C. Grease a standard 12-hole muffin tin well. In a bowl, whisk together the 250 g flour, 1.5 tsp baking powder, half a teaspoon bicarbonate of soda, 1.5 tsp cinnamon, half a teaspoon nutmeg and half a teaspoon salt. In a second bowl, whisk the 60 g melted butter, 120 g light brown sugar, 2 eggs, 80 ml buttermilk and the cooled apple reduction until combined. Pour the wet mix into the dry and fold together until just combined. Divide evenly among the muffin holes (about two-thirds full). Bake for 14 to 16 minutes until a skewer comes out clean and the tops spring back when pressed lightly. Cool in the tin for 5 minutes.

Mix the 80 g caster sugar and 1 tsp cinnamon in a shallow bowl. While the doughnuts are still warm, turn each one out of the tin, brush all over with the 30 g melted butter and roll in the cinnamon sugar until well coated. Place on a rack and serve warm or at room temperature within a few hours.

items.solution: The reduction was not cooked down enough. The finished reduction should taste intensely sweet and apple-concentrated. If it still tastes like juice, return it to the pan and reduce further.

items.problem: Doughnuts have a flat top rather than a dome
items.solution: The tin was not filled enough, or the oven temperature was too low. Fill each hole about two-thirds full and bake at 200 degrees C so the batter sets quickly and rises.
